cards.label: Summary
cards.text: Raspberry Jam Wattle is a woody shrub or tree recorded from about 1 to 7 metres high, occasionally reaching about 12 metres.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: Its yellow flowering display is recorded from July to October. Florabase notes that it occupies a broad variety of soils and habitats.

cards.label: Flowers / Cones / Fruit
cards.text: Yellow; Winter; Spring

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Recorded across varied soils and habitats rather than being restricted to one substrate type.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Florabase records a broad Western Australian range across numerous inland and south-western bioregions, including the Avon Wheatbelt, Jarrah Forest and Swan Coastal Plain.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Florabase lists the taxon as not threatened in Western Australia and as native in part of its range but naturalised elsewhere.

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Florabase name and descriptive fields from profile 3200 were curated into ArbotFlash on 17 June 2026. Australian Plant Census and global-backbone checks remain pending.

cards.label: Summary
cards.text: Coastal Wattle is usually a dense shrub and only occasionally a small tree, generally 0.8 to 4 metres high and sometimes about 7 metres.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: Florabase records yellow flowers and a dense coastal growth form. The selected diagnostic image shows the distinctive curved pods and conspicuous red-orange seed arils.

cards.label: Flowers / Cones / Fruit
cards.text: Yellow; Spring; Summer; Autumn

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Associated with white or grey sand, coastal dunes and limestone.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Native to Western Australia, especially coastal and near-coastal regions spanning the south-west and south coast.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Florabase lists the taxon as not threatened in Western Australia.

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Florabase name and descriptive fields from profile 3282 were curated into ArbotFlash on 17 June 2026. Australian Plant Census and global-backbone checks remain pending.

cards.label: Summary
cards.text: Silver Wattle is a shrub or tree recorded in Western Australia from about 1.5 to 30 metres high. Florabase treats it as alien to Western Australia.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: Florabase records smooth grey-brown bark that becomes deeply corrugated with age, glaucous foliage and yellow flowers.

cards.label: Bark
cards.text: Smooth; Fissured

cards.label: Flowers / Cones / Fruit
cards.text: Yellow; Winter; Spring

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Recorded on loamy or granitic soils on stony slopes and creekbanks.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: The Western Australian record is concentrated in the Jarrah Forest and Warren bioregions; the species originates in south-eastern Australia.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Florabase lists the taxon as not threatened but alien to Western Australia.

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Florabase profile 17858 was curated into ArbotFlash on 17 June 2026. National and global accepted-name reconciliation remains pending.

cards.label: Summary
cards.text: Blackwood is a tree, occasionally a shrub, reaching about 30 metres. Florabase treats Western Australian occurrences as alien.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: Florabase records yellow to cream flowers. The course name Blackwood is retained; fuller phyllode, bark and pod details still require a specialist identification source.

cards.label: Flowers / Cones / Fruit
cards.text: Yellow; Cream; Winter; Spring

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Recorded on gravelly lateritic soils, including wetland-associated settings in the south-west.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Recorded in the Jarrah Forest, Swan Coastal Plain and Warren bioregions; native range is eastern and south-eastern Australia.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Florabase lists the taxon as not threatened but alien to Western Australia.

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Florabase profile 10955 was curated into ArbotFlash on 17 June 2026. APC and global reconciliation remain pending.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Native to Western Australia and recorded across a broad set of south-western and inland bioregions.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Florabase lists the taxon as not threatened in Western Australia.

cards.label: Summary
cards.text: WA peppermint is a tree or shrub recorded by Florabase to about 10 metres high.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: The species has a characteristically weeping habit and white flowers; its foliage is aromatic when crushed.

cards.label: Flowers / Cones / Fruit
cards.text: White; Winter; Spring; Summer

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Recorded on white or grey sand, sandy soils, laterite and limestone in coastal dunes, granite outcrops and limestone areas.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Florabase records both native and naturalised occurrences in Western Australia.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Florabase lists the taxon as not threatened in Western Australia.

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Florabase name and descriptive fields were curated into ArbotFlash on 17 June 2026. The mixed establishment status is retained.

cards.label: Summary
cards.text: Western sheoak is a dioecious tree recorded by Florabase at about 5–15 metres high.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: The bark is fibrous and reddish-brown. Male and female reproductive structures occur on separate plants.

cards.label: Bark
cards.text: Fibrous; Reddish-brown

cards.label: Flowers / Cones / Fruit
cards.text: Brown; Autumn; Winter; Spring

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Recorded on lateritic soils and white, grey or yellow sands in jarrah forest and sand-dune habitats.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Native to Western Australia, particularly the Jarrah Forest, Swan Coastal Plain and Warren bioregions.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Florabase lists the taxon as not threatened in Western Australia.

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Florabase name and descriptive fields were curated into ArbotFlash on 17 June 2026.

cards.label: Status

cards.text: Variable glossy leaves and a stout trunk are useful field characters.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Native to Victoria, New South Wales and Queensland; planted more widely.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Native in stated range; Cultivated outside native range

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Wikipedia revision 1358523803 and Wikidata identifier Q947016 were recorded on 17 June 2026 as supplemental evidence. Catalogue of Life and specialist-authority review remain pending.

cards.label: Summary
cards.text: Crimson bottlebrush is an eastern Australian woody shrub or small tree widely cultivated for its vivid red flower spikes.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: Narrow aromatic leaves and cylindrical spikes dominated by long red stamens are characteristic; woody capsules often persist on older stems.

cards.label: Leaves / Branchlets
cards.text: Alternate

cards.label: Flowers / Cones / Fruit
cards.text: Red; Woody capsule

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Wetland margins; Creek lines; Woodland

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Native to eastern and south-eastern Australia and widely cultivated beyond its natural range.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Native in stated range

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Wikipedia revision 1355337215 and Wikidata Q6810997 were recorded as supplemental evidence. The POWO accepted-name mapping was recorded without changing the canonical course card.

cards.label: Summary
cards.text: White Cypress Pine is an erect tree recorded to about 7.6 metres high in the Florabase Western Australian profile.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: Blue-green foliage and rough grey bark that may be fissured or fibrous are useful regional field characters.

cards.label: Bark
cards.text: Fibrous; Fissured

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Recorded on varied sands, clays and loams as well as gypsum, gravel, ironstone, laterite, granite and quartzite, across landforms including sandplains, slopes, summits, valleys, cliffs, breakaways and salt-lake margins.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Florabase records a broad Western Australian distribution across south-western, arid and northern bioregions.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Florabase lists the species as not threatened, with mixed status: native in part of its Western Australian range and naturalised elsewhere.

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Florabase profile 8466 was curated into ArbotFlash on 17 June 2026. National and global backbone reconciliation remains queued.

cards.label: Summary
cards.text: River Sheoak is recorded by Florabase as a tree about 5 to 9 metres high in its Western Australian occurrences and is alien to the state.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: A sheoak canopy of fine jointed branchlets and a river- or creek-associated habit are key study cues; the reviewed image shows a flowering whole form.

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Florabase records loam over granite in eucalypt woodland at a creek edge.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Western Australian records occur in the Jarrah Forest and Swan Coastal Plain bioregions.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: The taxon is current in Florabase, not threatened and alien to Western Australia.

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Florabase profile 18313 was curated into ArbotFlash on 17 June 2026. More detailed cone and branchlet imagery remains pending.

cards.text: Camphor laurel is a large evergreen aromatic tree native to eastern and south-eastern Asia and planted widely elsewhere.

cards.text: Glossy alternate leaves often show three prominent veins from near the base; crushed tissues are strongly aromatic, and small dark drupes follow inconspicuous flowers.

cards.text: Alternate

cards.text: Drupe

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Forest; Urban plantings

cards.label: Distribution

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Native in stated range

cards.label: Data quality

cards.text: Corymbia haematoxylon is a Western Australian tree, or occasionally a mallee-like plant, recorded from about 2.5 to 15 metres high. Florabase uses Mountain Marri as a common name; the original course record is preserved as Bloodwood.

cards.label: Field ID

cards.text: Tessellated

cards.label: Flowers / Cones / Fruit
cards.text: White; Spring; Summer; Autumn

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Native to Western Australia, with records in the Geraldton Sandplains, Jarrah Forest and Swan Coastal Plain bioregions.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Florabase lists the taxon as not threatened in Western Australia.

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Florabase profile 17105 was curated into ArbotFlash on 17 June 2026. The course common name is retained, while the regional common-name difference is documented. APC and global-backbone checks remain pending.

cards.label: Summary
cards.text: Italian cypress is a long-lived evergreen conifer native from the eastern Mediterranean region toward Iran and widely planted as an ornamental.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: Dense scale-like foliage, rounded woody cones and a naturally narrow or broadly columnar crown are characteristic.

cards.label: Flowers / Cones / Fruit
cards.text: Cone

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Mediterranean woodland; Rocky slopes

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Native to parts of the eastern Mediterranean and western Asia; cultivated throughout temperate and Mediterranean-climate regions.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Native in stated range; Cultivated outside native range

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Wikipedia revision 1358336120 and Wikidata Q147513 were recorded as supplemental evidence. POWO accepted-name status was cross-checked on 17 June 2026.

authoritySource.citation: Western Australian Herbarium (1998–). Florabase—the Western Australian flora. Taxon profile 5545, accessed 17 June 2026.

cards.label: Summary
cards.text: Powderbark Wandoo is a tree generally 2.5 to 15 metres high and occasionally about 25 metres.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: Smooth pink-white bark is a defining field feature. Flowers are white to cream, sometimes with a yellow tone.

cards.label: Bark
cards.text: Smooth

cards.label: Flowers / Cones / Fruit
cards.text: White; Cream; Yellow; Summer; Autumn

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Usually found on lateritic breakaways or stony ridges in lateritic gravelly soils or clay loam.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Native to Western Australia and recorded in the Avon Wheatbelt, Geraldton Sandplains, Jarrah Forest and Swan Coastal Plain.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Florabase lists the taxon as not threatened in Western Australia.

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Florabase name and descriptive fields from profile 5545 were curated into ArbotFlash on 17 June 2026. Australian Plant Census and global-backbone checks remain pending.

species.acceptedNameStatus: Current in Florabase

authoritySource.citation: Western Australian Herbarium (1998–). Florabase—the Western Australian flora. Taxon profile 5580, accessed 17 June 2026.

cards.label: Summary
cards.text: River Red Gum is a tree generally recorded by Florabase from about 5 to 20 metres high in Western Australia.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: The trunk commonly has smooth, pale or powdery white bark. Florabase records white flowers.

cards.label: Bark
cards.text: Smooth

cards.label: Flowers / Cones / Fruit
cards.text: White; Winter; Spring; Summer

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Strongly associated with watercourses, billabongs and floodplain settings on alluvium, sand, rocky soil or deep red sand.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: The WA record includes native and naturalised occurrences across numerous inland and coastal bioregions.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Florabase lists the taxon as not threatened in Western Australia.

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Florabase profile 5580 was curated into ArbotFlash on 17 June 2026. Native-versus-naturalised status varies by occurrence and requires location-level interpretation; APC and global checks remain pending.

cards.label: Summary
cards.text: Sugar Gum is described in Florabase as a tree to about 15 metres high and alien to Western Australia.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: The regional description records smooth, mottled grey bark and a tree habit.

cards.label: Bark
cards.text: Smooth; Grey

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Western Australian records include shelly sand over clay and brown clay loam on flats and gentle slopes.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Florabase lists records in the Esperance Plains, Swan Coastal Plain and Warren bioregions.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: The species is current in Florabase, not threatened, and alien to Western Australia; its origin is south-eastern Australia.

cards.text: Karri is a very tall south-western eucalypt; Florabase records trees from about 10 metres to as much as 90 metres.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: The trunk is characteristically smooth-barked and the flowers are white.

cards.label: Bark
cards.text: Smooth

cards.label: Flowers / Cones / Fruit
cards.text: White; Autumn; Spring; Summer

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Recorded on sandy loam, particularly on hillsides.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Native to Western Australia, especially the high-rainfall south-west and Warren region.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Florabase lists the taxon as not threatened in Western Australia.

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Florabase name and descriptive fields were curated into ArbotFlash on 17 June 2026.

cards.label: Summary
cards.text: Tasmanian Blue Gum is a large tree recorded in Western Australia at about 20 to 45 metres high and treated by Florabase as alien.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: Florabase records smooth white bark. The reviewed image shows the glaucous buds, white flowers and adult leaves.

cards.label: Bark
cards.text: Smooth; White

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Recorded on sandy clay in eucalypt woodland, wetland and riverine-flat settings.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Western Australian records occur in the Esperance Plains, Jarrah Forest, Swan Coastal Plain and Warren bioregions; the species originates in south-eastern Australia.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Florabase lists the taxon as not threatened but alien to Western Australia.

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Florabase profile 18216 was curated into ArbotFlash on 17 June 2026. Subspecies treatment and national/global reconciliation remain pending.

cards.label: Summary
cards.text: Flooded Gum or Rose Gum is a very fast-growing tree that Florabase records as alien to Western Australia and capable of exceeding 50 metres.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: The regional management account describes a tall rainforest-margin tree with fibrous-flaky bark retained on the lower trunk and smooth pale bark above.

cards.label: Bark
cards.text: Smooth; Fibrous

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: In Western Australia it has been recorded escaping amenity plantings into disturbed edges of low-lying Banksia woodland.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Florabase records it on the Swan Coastal Plain, including Perth, with Armadale and Kalamunda local-government records.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Current in Florabase, not threatened, and alien to Western Australia; native to Queensland and New South Wales.

cards.label: Data quality
cards.text: Florabase profile 48440 was curated into ArbotFlash on 17 June 2026. The course common name Flooded Gum is retained.

cards.label: Summary
cards.text: Red Tingle is a very large south-western tree, commonly 8 to 55 metres high and recorded by Florabase to about 70 metres.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: The trunk has rough, deeply furrowed, stringy bark. The massive butt and fire-hollowed trunk can be conspicuous in mature trees. Florabase records white flowers.

cards.label: Bark
cards.text: Fibrous; Stringy; Fissured

cards.label: Flowers / Cones / Fruit
cards.text: White; Summer; Autumn

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Recorded on loam on hillslopes and in gullies.

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Native to Western Australia and concentrated in the Warren bioregion around the high-rainfall south coast.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Florabase lists the taxon as not threatened in Western Australia.

cards.text: Florabase lists the taxon as not threatened in Western Australia.

cards.label: Status

cards.label: Habitat

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Native to Western Australia and widespread through several south-western bioregions.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Florabase lists the taxon as not threatened in Western Australia.

cards.label: Data quality

cards.text: Native in stated range

cards.label: Summary
cards.text: Yellow bloodwood is a medium tree native to eastern New South Wales.

cards.label: Field ID
cards.text: Rough yellow-brown tessellated bark, lance-shaped adult leaves, pale flowers and urn-shaped woody capsules are useful characters.

cards.label: Bark
cards.text: Rough tessellated bark

cards.label: Flowers / Cones / Fruit
cards.text: Cream; White; Capsule

cards.label: Habitat
cards.text: Dry sclerophyll woodland; Rocky sandstone slopes

cards.label: Distribution
cards.text: Native to eastern New South Wales, commonly associated with sandstone country and dry sclerophyll woodland.

cards.label: Status
cards.text: Native in stated range
